standard_description: Form and use frequently occurring irregular plural nouns (e.g., feet, children, teeth, mice, fish).
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Irregular plural nouns: f to -ves plurals
khan_exercises.description: Identify and use plural nouns whose endings switch from "f" to "ves."
topic_path: Irregular plural nouns: base plurals and irregular endings
Irregular plural nouns: f to -ves plurals

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct plural noun to use in this sentence.**

Ryan and Sam went to lunch with their \_\_\_\_\_\_ every Saturday.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Often, singular nouns that end with an **"f"** change to **"ves"** when they become plural, like how the plural of **"leaf"** is **"leaves"**.

There are some exceptions to this trend. For example, the plural of **"chef"** is **"chefs"**.

Unfortunately, there's no absolute rule: we have to learn through experience which words follow this pattern.
The plural of **"wife"** is **"wives"**.

khan_exercises.title: Irregular plural nouns: -en plurals
khan_exercises.description: A few nouns become plural by adding -en at the end.
topic_path: Irregular plural nouns: base plurals and irregular endings

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct plural noun to fill in the blank in the sentence.**

On Sundays I like to play baseball with my \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Only a handful of plural nouns end in **-en** rather than the typical **-s** ending. These include words like **children** and **oxen**. There are others, but they're old and out of use.
The plural form of "child" is "**children**". It's one of the weird ones!

**Box** may rhyme with **ox**, but it's a regular plural noun. The plural of **box** is **boxes**.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: A few plural nouns have endings that include -en, rather than the typical -s plural ending.

The nouns in English that use -en as their plural endings are:

-children

-oxen

khan_exercises.title: Irregular plural nouns: the base plural
khan_exercises.description: Some nouns are exactly the same in both their singular and plural forms. These are them!
topic_path: Irregular plural nouns: base plurals and irregular endings
Irregular plural nouns: the base plural

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct plural for the singular noun "sheep".**

Three \_\_\_\_\_\_ grazed in the meadow.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Remember: this exercise is about **base plurals**. 

A base plural is the *same* as the singular form of the noun.

Examples of irregular base plurals: **sheep, fish, deer, aircraft.** There's no trick to these except to memorize them.
The plural of **sheep** is **sheep**. It's a base plural!

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct plural for the singular noun "salmon".**

\_\_\_\_\_\_ live in the ocean, but each year they swim upstream to reproduce in freshwater rivers.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Remember: this exercise is about **base plurals**. 

A base plural is the *same* as the singular form of the noun.

Examples of irregular base plurals: **sheep, fish, deer, aircraft.** There's no trick to these except to memorize them.

The plural of **salmon** is **salmon**. It's a base plural!

**Note:** "Salmons" *could* be acceptable in some rare cases, usually when scientists are talking about multiple species of salmon.

khan_exercises.title: Irregular plural nouns: mutant plurals
khan_exercises.description: Some nouns become plural by changing their vowels instead of adding an -s.
topic_path: Irregular plural nouns: mutant and foreign plurals

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct plural for the noun "mouse".**

 \_\_\_\_\_\_ love this cheese! 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
English has a few “mutant” plural nouns whose vowel sounds change when they become plural. The mutant plurals are:

woman/women 

man/men

louse/lice

mouse/mice

tooth/teeth

foot/feet

goose/geese

There's no way around it except to memorize them!
The plural of **mouse** is **mice**. It's a mutant plural!

There's no way around it except to memorize them!
The plural of **foot** is **feet**. It's a mutant plural!

hints:
English has a few “mutant” plural nouns whose vowel sounds change when they become plural. The mutant plurals are:

woman/women 

man/men

louse/lice

mouse/mice

tooth/teeth

foot/feet

goose/geese

There's no way around it except to memorize them!
The plural of **tooth** is **teeth**. It's a mutant plural!

khan_exercises.title: Irregular plural nouns: foreign plurals
khan_exercises.description: Identify nouns borrowed from other languages and their plural forms!
topic_path: Irregular plural nouns: mutant and foreign plurals

items.question_content:
**Choose the correct plural for the noun "virus".**

There are so many dangerous \_\_\_\_\_\_ in this laboratory; be careful, that one will melt your liver.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
English has several borrowed words from other languages (such as Latin and Greek) that result in irregular plurals. Some examples are listed below:

- Index/indices
- Cactus/cacti
- Larva/larvae
- Crisis/crises
- bacterium/bacteria

Unfortunately, there's no exact rule we can follow; we need to learn foreign plurals through our experience with words.
**"Virus"** uses the standard **-es** plural ending. The plural form is **"viruses"**.

**"Radius"** has a foreign plural. The plural form is **"radii"**.
